speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the [? Csorna? ?] Oh, during the forest. Well, we have to build our own-- where we could lodge. We digged holes in the ground and make one from wood-- and on the top of the wood, dirt-- so for our roof. And we were out of there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --and that was-- well, we have several [? warden. ?] There was a kitchen over there. Food-- we didn't have much. Early in the morning, we had our-- just a plain, black coffee without sugar, without [LAUGHS] cream, or milk, or something. And for lunch, we got some soup-- some kinda good soup. And in the evening, we have some-- some vegetables, or potatoes, or something-- sometimes with some meat. But many people didn't eat their meat. And so it was terrible. It was terrible. We really-- we worked very hard. And we were always hungry. And we were so weak. And-- and-- we-- there was a little river or something by-- was over there-- there where we washed ourself clean, or some washed their clothes, and on. Some said there a terrible situation over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you keep religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people-- yes, yes-- who want. Many people, they didn't eat [? there ?] [? truthfully. ?] And we have services every morning, in the evening. So if somebody saw that and he wants to join the service, they don't want to also joined over there. And we prayed together. And so we-- we keep up pretty good. I put on the tefillin every morning-- well, no matter who wants to put on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the commander in chief?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the commander-- [SIGHS] well, we have a-- the last commander before-- there was a commander. He wasn't very mean. He wasn't good. He wasn't mean. He didn't care [? much. ?] And from an accident, he end up in a hospital. And from the headquarter, they sended out a-- a lead officer, a young man. He was a Seventh-day Adventist, and he was very good. It was simple in-- in-- in-- in the food. It was improving in the work. It was-- everything was much better. He really take good care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Kubini-- Zoltan Kubini.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the picture of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one that got sick-- his name? The one that got sick? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember his name. That was now 50-- 52, 53 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to your group when the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: --invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there, we were working-- we were working at the Bryansk Forest. One day came the order from the German headquarter to take this group to Germany, to the concentrations camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this officer, Kubini, took us back to Hungary, to our homeland. And we had an opportunity in Hungary to work. And-- where-- how we were-- were-- we were-- we were marching. We pass by a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And officer came. This commander officer went into a farm and make an arrangement with the people over there. There wasn't any people who would work there in the-- in the farm, because everybody was in the service. There was a-- an old couple, and-- and I think the daughter was over there. And they needed people to work for them, to-- there were-- the field was outside-- corn, and potatoes, and vegetables-- everything, though they needed people who would work. And they'd say, pick up those and bring 'em in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he made an arrangement. We would work over there. And he give us lodge-- lodging, and-- and the food. So we were there for a couple-- maybe three, four weeks. So what was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: --name of the place where--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I can't remember this place-- but it was near Miskolc, a big town in Hungary-- Miskolc. And it was not far from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was about September, I remember. It was about September. It was in-- in 1944, September. And one day, some military police showed up-- probably somebody reported to the headquarter there is some Jewish people are there, and-- I think-- or something. And he had the order to took us to Germany-- yeah, but to Germany, to the concentrations camp. So our commander officer couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were marching to-- to-- toward Germany. We were marching about three, four days. We were always lodging in some-- some empty building or some store. So one evening, that night, we wake up. And our commander officer came in and asked be quiet. So what happened? They [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] get some wine. They make drunk the police. The police-- the-- the-- the those [INAUDIBLE]-- the-- those guards who took it-- who would-- would taken us to-- to the concentration camp, to Germany. And they were drunk. And they were sleep-- deep in sleep. So we get up. And we were-- we were marching against-- against-- against the-- where we-- where we-- we supposed to go to-- to Germany. And we were walking a couple days. And we arrived to a big city-- Balassagyarmat, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there we could hear the-- bomb-- the-- the military noise. So the-- the Germans was close over there-- the Russian. The Russian was close over there. And we could hear the military noise over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our commander officer took care of us. He based us in different cellars, in different shelters. And next day, the Russian came in. And we were liberated. So we heard some noise in the morning. We came out from the shelter, from the-- the cellar. And we saw strange soldiers. They were Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they-- they-- they knew who we are, because they saw we had our yellow armband with the number of-- for-- where we belonged over them. So they know who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number was 108 plus 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody had the same number?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody had the same. This group had the same number. Different guy has a different number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to when you were marching to Germany, to the concentration camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't go to the Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on the way there, on the way to Germany, when they told you you had to go to the concentration camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] we-- we-- many people thinked about it, to escape. But they-- we couldn't. We couldn't, because there was 8, 10 military policemen who was around out. And nobody could escape there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So then when the Russian came in, and we were liberated, and-- and for a couple days, we were there. Sorry to interrupt you, but to go back a little-- what was the hardest thing about being in this labor battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hunger. We was so hungry. Oh, if we didn't have any food, we were so weak-- hungry-- we-- and then hard labor. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact-- sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, we got bread-- what was burned. And a friend of mine, he had some stomach trouble, and he couldn't eat the-- the-- this bread-- the the-- this top of the bread where it burned. So he cut it off, this one, thinking, give it to me. It was so bitter, but it was so delicious. I remember that. And I was always thinking just one-- I could eat enough bread. It was terrible. This-- they-- they-- they was-- they was starving. That was the most terrible thing-- the starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the winter-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, everybody had some-- well, warm clothing. And everybody had a blanket. So we somehow, we helped each other. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know when the Germans invaded Hungary? Were you aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were somewhere in there. They were gone when the German occupied Hungary. I wasn't home that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that affect the way you were treated? Did it affect the way you were treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand this question.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --invaded Hungary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the way you were treated in this battalion, is it affected? Were you treated better--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- no. It-- well, it-- it did. I think it didn't make any difference, because when the-- when we went to [? Csorna, ?] then the German, he-- the commanding office took us over. And we got the orders from the German. So it wasn't any different from there. There we got the order from the-- from the Hungarian military commander, or the German commander. The food wasn't any different. It was the-- the same. And the labor was the same-- hard work. And so it wasn't any different. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened behind in the country, in Hungary? We didn't know-- absolutely nothing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] How did you feel about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt I'm Jewish. And I raised up religious. And in the camp, I did pray every morning and every evening. And I make-- if I eat something, I made a blessing. Just-- I was accustomed all my life at home. And most of the people was-- did the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So could you continue and describe me how you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So when we were marching back, we arrive to a city-- Balassagyarmat. And that was-- we heard the noise of the military activities. So everybody-- the-- the people of the town, they were hiding in cellars and in shelters. So our commander officer also took care of us and took us different shelter, different cellars. And we were there during the night. And we'd see violent people. And everybody was scared, very much, because we heard the noise. We was very scared. And early in the morning, we heard some noise, some strange noise, strange-- not Hungarian talking, something-- something like that. So we came off of-- we came out from the shelter, and we send-- we saw strange soldiers. Even though those are Russian soldier, we didn't see before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they know who we are. And they told us, just we are looking for-- for soldiers, for Hungarian, Germans. We're looking for. Is there anything? Not here. So they went from house to house. And-- and they-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, they took us-- the-- the soldiers, they take-- came some other soldiers, and took us to a big town square. And they separated the people. They separated the civilian people. And they separated people who were-- who were in military uniform. The soldiers-- so many soldiers were hiding over there. But they changed. They clothed us in civilian-- for civilian clothes. Because by the Russian, if you in military clothes, everybody who's in military clothes-- it's a enemy. And we were ask our commander officer, please, change your clothes to-- to-- to civilian clothes. No, he said. He was a very honest man. He said, I don't have nothing to hide. I'm-- I'm-- I'm a member of the Hungarian army. I served my country honestly. I saved these 100-- 40, 50 people-- I don't have nothing to hide-- and very proud of that. And we were begging, please. The Russian wouldn't accept this. No, no. I'm-- I'm not afraid of that. So he was in uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they-- they-- they came-- they came to the group where there were people in the uniform. And later we heard the-- that they took them to-- to Siberia. And he was there in a labor camp. He was working in a labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple years later, we heard he passed away over there. He died over there. But that what happened. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then we find out he survive with a young child-- lives in Budapest-- and very hard condition. So some people who were in this group, and lived in Budapest, they organized, in every month-- or week, or two weeks-- somebody sended for the-- for this woman, a care package. I send that. My turn was maybe two or three times. And every time when I send her the package, I had a nice thank you note from this woman. Now, the last-- and we send them a picture of her husband, of commanding officer. The last package, when I sent her, she sended thank you note. And she asked me not to send anymore, because he got a nice job, and he can support himself and his son. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And several years ago, when the Yad Vashem was organized, that was in the '90s-- and I remember that. And I thought, this man would deserve to remember him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I wrote that down, the whole story of what happened, and how it happened, and how-- how she saved our live, those 150, 160 people, how he served.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in English, I am not so perfect. So mine daughter helped me. She translated in English. And I send it into the Yad Vashem-- took it to Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: And other two friends, who was-- we were together in the camp-- I had a paper to sign. This-- this story is true. And they were-- we were together in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it took it about two-- two years till the Yad Vashem-- oh. And I didn't-- I was looking for his-- there-- for-- for this woman, for the wife of this commander officer. And we find out from Budapest, they move to Miskolc. But we didn't know the house-- the-- the address. We know it's Kubini.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were looking in the telephone book. There was two Kubini in the telephone book in Miskolc. And we call up. And one-- one of 'em, yes, I'm-- I'm the-- I'm the son of this-- this-- it's old-time Kubini. But my mother passed away several years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I said, I recommended to the-- to the Yad Vashem and described, he's not-- of course, he's not alive, but his son is here. And he would deserve some recognition. So they asked paper to-- to prove he is really his son. He have to send that in, the birth certificate from the father, and his certificate. So it was so much we did. And I wouldn't say that's so much, because I was happy to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then, finally it came. There was a recognition day in Budapest. And they call up this young Kubini. His name is Martin Kubini-- call up, and asked him to come in Budapest, and with a nice-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they presented him with a certificate and a nice medal for his father. I didn't see the medal all the way. I-- I-- but my daughter was there-- not in that time. But he was in Tokaj, and he meeted this young man, and he-- she saw this medal-- medal and-- and the certificate. So that's how it was. That's all I could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now going back to where you-- what day were you liberated? What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was in the first part of December, '44. I can't remember exactly the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the next-- then we have to work a couple days. We took us to a-- to a next town. It was hot one. It was a big sugar factory over there. We didn't-- we didn't work in the sugar factory, but we were loading from-- in-- some food in-- in-- in big-- the-- boxes and sack. And we were later loading this from the railroad track and into a warehouse. That's what we did for a couple days till we get our-- our papers-- are we free. And we can go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you feel in that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so happy, and we were so anxious to-- to-- to arrive to our hometown. And we know that nobody were over there, but we could hear some things what happened with our dear ones. And we were five in this group. There wasn't any-- any-- train or railroad over there to-- we could travel. So we just walked. We walked for days-- three days. Yeah, more this-- it was several days. We-- we finally-- we arrived in our town. And there was some Jewish people who came back, some-- also from-- from-- from different labor camp. There was 30 people over there. And they were staying in somewhere. We arrived at night. And we went over there. And we stay with them. And next day, what we did-- so there wasn't any Jewish people where-- in-- there were-- was in many empty houses over there. So most of them was in very bad condition. And everywhere, they empty. They-- they-- [LAUGHS] they took care of the-- the people over there. They took away everything-- furniture. There wasn't any. So we find one Jewish-- Jewish house. It were pretty good shape. So we settled then down over there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so we happen-- we-- we have to do our living, so we did some business in the black market. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People bought some-- some people bought some oil. That was very, very valuable, because there wasn't any-- any-- the-- the-- the butter, or margarine-- anything. That was only oil for cooking-- and took it to Budapest, or some other big city, where it was-- we liberated, and sold it, through that time. What I made-- what I made a living, there was a-- a-- I made cigarettes. I bought from the farmers tobacco, and cut it real fine, and-- and made cigarette; and put all the cigarette on a little box. And I don't know how there-- 200 [? bale. ?] And I still took it to Budapest. And I sold it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was pharmacy-- there was a pharmacy in the town. He said, I need medicine-- anything. Why don't you try to get some medicine in Budapest? So I solded the cigarette in Budapest. But I would made, I-- what I made-- and for the money, I bought some medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I brought it down to-- to-- to Tokaj. And I sold it to their pharmacy. I did it for a while. And somebody warned me-- don't do that. Because you sell some medicine, and somebody may die from that. You are in big trouble. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So meantime, Ilona came back. And we were married. And I opened my family business, the-- the-- we-- we-- yeah. And we'll continue in the next tape.
